coredata.dc:title: Enhanced pseudocapacitive performance of MoS2 by introduction of both N-GQDs and HCNT for flexible supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Hao
Guo, Zengsheng
Li, Ming
Hu, Guanghui
Tang, Tao
Wen, Jianfeng
Li, Xinyu
Huang, Haifu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report a strategy for enhancing the pseudocapacitive performance of MoS2 materials by the introduction of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and helical carbon tubes (HCNT). By a simple hydrothermal process, the hybrid composite (MoS2, N-GQDs and HCNT) were coated on the carbon cloth (CC) without any binders, which can be directly used as advanced electrode material for high-performance solid-state flexible supercapacitors. In this hybrid structure, N-GQDs are inserted between the layers of MoS2 nanosheets, which induce and expose more active sites for pseudocapacitance contributions in MoS2 electrode materials. Meanwhile, HCNT acts as electrically conducting bridge for enhancing the electrical conductivity of MoS2 nanosheets and speeding up transport of electronic. As results, the hybrid composite (MoS2, N-GQDs and HCNT) show higher capacitive performance than pure MoS2. The specific capacitance of hybrid composite is high up to 3360 mF cm−2. Further, the assembled solid-state flexible supercapacitor shows high specific capacitance of 1893 mF cm−2, outstanding cycle life (capacitance retention of 86% after 2500 cycles), good mechanical stability and great flexibility. Our work provides a reference for the preparation of MoS2 composites with high supercapacitive performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
MoS2 nanosheets
Helical carbon nanotubes
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical performance in a two-electrode system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC material.

(a, b and c) SEM images of MoS2/CC at different reaction temperature (160 °C, 180 °C and 200 °C) and reaction time of 20 h, (d, e and f) SEM images of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC at reaction temperature of 200 °C with different reaction time (16, 20 and 24 h).
TEM and HRTEM images: (a, b) N-GQDs, (c, d, e and f) MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC.
(a) XRD patterns of pure CC, HCNTs, N-GQDs, MoS2/CC and MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC, (b) Raman spectra of CC, MoS2/CC and MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of the MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC, (b) S 2p, (c) Mo 3d, (d) N 1 s, (e) C 1 s.

(a) CV curves of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC,MoS2@CC, MoS2/HCNT@CC and CC electrodes at 10 mV s−1, (b) CV curves of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC, (c) GCD curves of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC; Comparison of specific capacitance: (d) MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC, MoS2/N-GQDs@CC and MoS2@CC, (e) MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC with different reaction time (16, 20 and 24 h), (f) MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC with different mass ratio of HCNT and N-GQDs (1:1, 1:2 and 1:3), (g, h) the corresponding Nyquist plots, (i) cyclic stability of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC at 30 mA cm−2.
(a) the capacitive contributions shown in the CV curve at 0.1 mV s−1, (b) the percentage of two contributions at different scan rates.

Electrochemical performance characteristics of the MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC ASSC device: (a) CV curves at different voltage window from 0 to 1.2 V at 10 mV s−1, (b) CV curves, (c) GCD curves, (d) specific capacitance of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC and MoS2@CC ASSC device, (e) cyclic stability of ASSC device at 25 mA cm−2, (f) the comparison of Nyquist plots before and after cycling.

(a) Schematic illustration for fabrication of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC ASSC device, (b) ASSC in different bending states. (c, d) CV curves of ASSC device various bending states at 10 mV s−1. (e) Ragone plot, (f) A LED powered by the ASSC device.
The experimental parameters for synthesizing MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC.
Capacitances of MoS2 based materials for comparison.

head.ce:title: Enhanced pseudocapacitive performance of MoS2 by introduction of both N-GQDs and HCNT for flexible supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science & Ministry-Province Jointly-Constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-Ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science & Ministry-Province Jointly-Constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-Ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Science & Ministry-Province Jointly-Constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-Ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Novel Battery Materials Research Center of Engineering Technology, Center on Nanoenergy Research, School of Physics Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Novel Battery Materials Research Center of Engineering Technology, Center on Nanoenergy Research, School of Physics Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bGuangxi Novel Battery Materials Research Center of Engineering Technology, Center on Nanoenergy Research, School of Physics Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report a strategy for enhancing the pseudocapacitive performance of MoS2 materials by the introduction of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and helical carbon tubes (HCNT). By a simple hydrothermal process, the hybrid composite (MoS2, N-GQDs and HCNT) were coated on the carbon cloth (CC) without any binders, which can be directly used as advanced electrode material for high-performance solid-state flexible supercapacitors. In this hybrid structure, N-GQDs are inserted between the layers of MoS2 nanosheets, which induce and expose more active sites for pseudocapacitance contributions in MoS2 electrode materials. Meanwhile, HCNT acts as electrically conducting bridge for enhancing the electrical conductivity of MoS2 nanosheets and speeding up transport of electronic. As results, the hybrid composite (MoS2, N-GQDs and HCNT) show higher capacitive performance than pure MoS2. The specific capacitance of hybrid composite is high up to 3360 mF cm−2. Further, the assembled solid-state flexible supercapacitor shows high specific capacitance of 1893 mF cm−2, outstanding cycle life (capacitance retention of 86% after 2500 cycles), good mechanical stability and great flexibility. Our work provides a reference for the preparation of MoS2 composites with high supercapacitive performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have been widely got attention because of their high power density, long cycle life and high safety factor [1]. With the rapid development of wearable electronic devices, it is required for supercapacitors in terms of flexibility and mechanical properties. To achieve the flexibility of supercapacitor, the electrodes must have the ability to withstand mechanical deformation while ensuring energy storage efficiency. There are mainly two routes to achieve this goal: one is to coat active materials on a flexible substrate using binder, and the other is to take active materials to self-assembly on the flexible current collector without any binder [2,3]. In the past research, the method had been explored to coat the electrode powder material on the current collector using the binder, but the electrode material was easy to fall off flexible substrate in the process of repeated charge and discharge. At the same time, the binder increases the resistance of the material, resulting in poor flexibility and lower energy density of the device [4]. Therefore, self-supporting nanoarrays grown directly on flexible substrates have become an attractive strategy for preparing flexible supercapacitors [5,6].

It's worth noting that the energy storage performance of flexible supercapacitors is mainly affected by electrode materials. Therefore, pseudocapacitive materials with high performance are developed gradually [7]. In various types of electrode materials, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is a new two-dimensional material worthy of attention and widely used in various fields such as Li-ion battery, dyesensitized solar cells, electrocatalysis and supercapacitors [8-10]. MoS2 consists of Mo and S, which possess high charge carrier transport due to its graphene-like two-dimensional structure. Such a structure can greatly reduce the energy barrier and facilitate ion insertion and transportation. Therefore, MoS2 exhibits excellent capacitive performance as electrode materials of supercapacitor [11–16]. For example, Chen et al. reported that flower-like MoS2 nanosheets had high specific capacitance (2236.6 mF cm−2 at 10 mA cm−2) and outstanding cyclic stability (capacitance retention: 86.1% after 2000 cycles, 10 mA cm−2) [17]. Islam et al. reported that MoS2 nanosheets assembled on microfiber paper showed a high specific capacitance of 125 mF cm−2 and a high power density of 12.05 W cm−3 [18]. Fu et al. showed that layered MoS2/graphene nanosheets prepared by electrospinning process can achieve a high specific capacity of 334 F g−1 (at 0.5 A g−1), which is higher than pure MoS2 electrode [19]. Sabeeh et al. reported a novel MoS2@RGO dual nanocomposite, which exhibited a high specific capacitance of 441 F g−1 with 85% retention for 1000 cycles [20]. However, the low exposure rate of the interlayer reaction site, high resistance and unideal structural stability lead to the low specific capacitance and poor cycle stability. To address its shortcomings, the combination of MoS2 and carbon with novel microstructure is effective strategies to optimize electrochemical performance [21].

In this work, we designed and synthesized of MoS2, N-GQDs and HCNT nanocomposites by a hydrothermal method. MoS2, N-GQDs and HCNT were directly grown on the carbon cloth to form a vertical hetero structure. N-GQDs can easily realize the surface functionalization of different groups at the edge sites, which provides pseudo-capacitance and changes the wettability of electrode materials. When inserted into the layers of MoS2 nanosheets, N-GQDs can effectively boost pseudocapacitance contributions of MoS2 electrode materials. Meanwhile, HCNT can be used as the electronic channel layer and growth substrate for the MoS2 nanosheets. As expect, hybrid composite (MoS2, N-GQDs and HCNT) show higher capacitive performance than pure MoS2.

Anhydrous sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4), nitric acid (HNO3) and thiourea (CH4N2S) were purchased from McLean industrial company, citric acid (C10H15O3) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with mean molecular weight of 1700 were purchased from Aladdin industrial company, carbon cloth with thickness of 0.41 mm was purchased from carbon industrial company, helical carbon nanotubes (HCNT) with length of 1~10 μm and outer diameter of 100~200 nm was purchased from Abundant Nano industrial company. All reagents were analytical pure grade, all aqueous solution preparation in deionized water (resistivity of 17.3 MΩ cm, temperature of 26 °C).

Based on previously reported work [22], a hydrothermal method was used to prepare N-GQDs. Firstly, the thiourea (0.72 g) used as N source and citric acid (0.84 g) used as C source were dissolved in 20 ml deionized water and kept stirring for 15 min. Secondly, the obtained mixed solution was transferred to the inner lining of a 50 ml reactor and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. Thirdly, the upper aqueous solution is collected by centrifuging method, and then dialyzed all nights inside 3500 MW dialysis membrane. Finally, N-GQDs were obtained by freeze-drying the above products.

In order to functionalize the carbon nanotubes, mixed acid treatment was used. In the experiment, the ratio of sulfuric acid and nitric acid was 1: 1. It was placed in the lining of a 100 mL reaction kettle. After a temperature of 160 °C for 8 h, the CNT was acidified to bring more groups for the active site by acid steam, and then washed with ethanol and deionized water alternately three times, dried for use.

The MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC flexible electrode was synthesized via a hydrothermal method, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . In a typical experiment, HCNT (50 mg), N-GQDs (50 mg), Na2MoO4 powder (1.21 g) and CH4N2S (1.56 g) were mixed evenly. The mixed solution and CC were then transferred to the liner of the reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 24 h. After hydrothermal heating of the reactor, the samples were taken out and washed alternately with alcohol and deionized water for several times, and then dried at 80 °C for 24 h. For comparison, MoS2/CC nanocomposites and MoS2/HCNT/CC were prepared using a similar method. To investigate the effects of heating time (16, 20 and 24 h) and heating temperature (160, 180 and 200 °C) on the microstructure and electrochemical performance of the obtained samples, the corresponding experimental process were carried out according to the experimental parameters in the Table 1 . The load mass of the active materials on CC is 2~4 mg cm−2.

The morphologies and compositions of the as-prepared samples were observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI SU5000) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS, INCA MAX-50). The compositions of the samples were further characterized using Raman (SENTERRA II) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS, ESCALAB-250XI). The phase structure of the obtained samples was confirmed by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Mini Flex 600) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ=1.5419 Å) ranging from 10° to 70°.

The electrochemical performance of the as-prepared samples as working electrode was carried out in the electrochemical workstation (CHI760E) using a three electrode system while the platinum (Pt) foil and Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode. 1 M Na2SO4 were used as electrolyte. The area specific capacitance was calculated based on the discharge curves.
(1)
C a
=
( I *
Δ
t )
/
( S *
Δ
V )
where Ca is the specific capacitance (mF cm−2), I is the test current (A), ∆t is discharge time (s), S is the geometric area of working electrode (cm2), and ∆V is the potential range. In order to evaluate the application potential of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC materials, all-solid-state symmetric supercapacitors (ASSC) device was assembled using MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC. For the ASSC device, the specific capacitances were calculated using the above equations (1). E (the average energy density, μW h cm−2) and P (the average power density, μW cm−2) can be calculated from the following formula [23]:
(2)
E = 1 / 2 *
C a
*
Δ
V 2
(3)
P = E /
Δ
t

Hydrothermal condition can trigger the reaction between the precursor and sulfur, resulting in the growth of MoS2 nanosheets on the surface of the carbon cloth [24–26]. In general, these nanoparticles further undergo intense heating process, which will promote the collision and aggregation of N-GQDs and HCNT with MoS2 molecules. The layered structure of MoS2 can provide larger specific surface area and more reaction sites, which is beneficial to the recombination of HCNT and N-GQDs with a MoS2 [27]. To determine the optimum condition of the preparation technology, the morphology of the MoS2/CC at different hydrothermal reaction temperatures (160 °C, 180 °C and 200 °C) and MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC with different reaction times (16, 20 and 24 h) was studied by SEM. The corresponding SEM images are shown in Fig. 2 . Clearly, the carbon cloth was tightly connected with a bundle of staggered carbon fibers, as inserted in Fig. 2a. When the hydrothermal reaction temperature is 160 °C and 180 °C, MoS2 covered on the framework of CC shows serious agglomeration phenomenon and no layered structure (Fig. 2a), resulting in lower reaction specific surface area of MoS2 [18-30]. Based on previously work [31], the nucleation rate can be improved as reaction temperature increases. These nanoparticles further underwent a further intense heating process, which promoted the formation of molecular collisions and aggregations. As shown in Fig. 2c, it found that MoS2 nanosheets can grow uniformly on the surface of CC at a higher temperature of 200 °C. Clearly, a higher reaction temperature is beneficial to obtain nanocomposites with good crystallinity and uniform distribution. Therefore, the hybrid composite MoS2/N-GQDs/HCNT on the surface of CC was prepared at 200 °C based on the above results. It's worth noting that the reaction time is critical. The morphology of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC at reaction temperature of 200 °C with different reaction time (16, 20 and 24 h) was provided in Fig. 2d-f for comparison. When reaction time is 16 and 20 h, hybrid composite MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC failed to grow uniformly on the surface of CC (Fig. 2d-e). However, under hydrothermal conditions with reaction time of 24 h, a large number of MoS2 nanosheets cover vertically and uniformly on the surface of CC, as shown in Fig. 2f. N-GQDs and HCNT are effectively compounded with MoS2 nanosheets. According to the above analysis, the reaction temperature of 200 °C and reaction time of 24 h are the optimum condition for the preparation of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC. The mass ratio of HCNT and N-GQD on the synthesis and electrochemical performance was also explored, as shown in Table 1. According to the research, when the mass ratio of HCNT: N-GQDs is 1:1, MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC shows best electrochemical performance.

The elemental mapping of the as-prepared MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC is shown in Fig. 3 . It can observe that the nitrogen element is evenly distributed in the MoS2 nanosheet layers, and the distribution of Mo and S elements clearly shows the skeleton of the MoS2. TEM was used to microstructure of as-prepared MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC. The size distribution of N-GQDs is ranging from 2.0 to 3.5 nm, confirmed by the Fig. 4 a. HRTEM shows that the d-spacing is 0.21 nm (Fig. 4b), corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphene. Fig. 4c-d shows that N-GQDs are inserted into the layer of MoS2 nanosheet gaps. In HRTEM images of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC (Fig. 4e-f), the d-spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.7 nm is corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphene and (002) crystal plane of MoS2, which is bigger than that of interlayer spacing of bulk MoS2 (0.62 nm). This expansion of the interlayer spacing can be attributed to the in-situ intercalation of ammonium ions hydrolyzed by thiourea into the molybdenum disulfide intermediate layer, affecting the formation of the MoS2 phase [32,33].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique was used to analyze the crystallinity of the as-prepared electrode material. As shown in Fig. 5 a, after adding N-GQDs and HCNT, there are obvious peaks at approximately 24° and 43°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphene [34]. Meanwhile, the diffraction peaks at 14.33°, 33.41°and 58.58°are attributed to the (002), (101) and (110) crystal planes of MoS2. Furthermore, pure CC show the weak peak centered at 25.7°, which may because the surface of CC was completely covered by uniform MoS2 nanosheets [35]. Meanwhile, compared with MoS2 prepared at the reaction time of 16 and 20 h, it was found that MoS2 prepared at the reaction time of 24 h has more sharp of the peak shape and better crystallinity.

The Raman spectra of MoS2 /HCNT/N-GQDs @CC, MoS2/CC and CC are shown in Fig. 5b for comparison. The bands of pure MoS2 can be observed at 404 and 378 cm−1, which belong to the out-of-plane A1g and in-plane E2g vibrational modes, respectively. The carbon cloth shows two sharp peaks at 1355 and 1588 cm−1. However, MoS2/CC there no any peaks of the carbon cloth, due to a layer of MoS2 nanosheets covered on the surface of carbon cloth. However, when MoS2/N-GQDs/HCNT coated on the carbon cloth, two peaks at1355 and 1588 cm−1 can be observed again, indicating that there are N-GQDs and HCNT compounded on the surface of MoS2 nanosheets [36,37]. Two peaks at 1355 and 1588 cm−1 are ascribed to the D and G band of disordered carbons and ideal graphitic sp2 carbons. Furthermore, the ratios of ID/IG band for carbon cloth and MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC are 1.09 and 0.92, respectively. MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC has a lower ID/IG ratio than that of carbon cloth, which indicates the increase in sp2 domains in MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC [38].

XPS was used to investigate the valence states and chemical composition of the MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC. The elementals Mo, S, N and C in MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC are confirmed by the survey XPS spectrum, which can be clearly observed in Fig. 6 a. In S 2p spectrum (Fig. 6b), two peaks at 161.73 and 162.95 eV are ascribed to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively. Furthermore, the Mo 3d spectrum (Fig. 6c) shows two peaks at 229.0 and 232.2 eV, which belong to the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively [39,40]. The high resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 6d) shows three peaks located at 399.1, 399.7 and 401.5 eV, which can be fitted into the pyridone N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively. The presence of graphene N indicates that the nitrogen atoms have successfully replaced the carbon atoms of the graphene layer, resulting in a high electrical conductivity of the material and good hydrophilia of the electrode surface. Meanwhile, the peaks of C 1s at 288.84, 286.59 and 284.94 eV (Fig. 6e) corresponding to the C–S, C–O and C–C bonds, respectively. Among them, the presence of C–S bond means the interface connection is through the covalent band, confirming the effective combination of HCNT, N-GQDs and MoS2 nanosheets [41].

The electrochemical performances of the MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC, MoS2/HCNT@CC and MoS2/CC electrodes were evaluated through cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) using 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte in a three-electrode system. The CV tests were carried out within a potential ranging from −0.8 to 0 V. As shown in the Fig. 7 a, MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC exhibits a larger area of CV curve than that of other samples at a same scan rate (10 mV s−1), implying that MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC possesses a higher areal specific capacitance. It's worth noting that the contribution of CC to the total capacitance is negligible when compared with MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC electrodes. Furthermore, as shown in the Fig. 7b, with the gradual increase of scanning speed, the CV curve shape of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC keeps the same trend, suggesting an excellent rate capability. Therefore, it is a positive effect for the abundant oxygen-containing groups in N-GQDs that provide a large additional pseudocapacitance for the charge storage [42].

The GCD curves of the MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC show an obvious quasi-triangular shape, as shown in Fig. 7c. Considering the nature characteristics of the electrode materials, the energy storage behavior should be the combination of double layer and pseudo capacitance. In the discharge curves, there is a small internal resistance (IR) drop. The specific capacitances (Ca ) of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC are calculated by Eq. (1) to be 3360, 3162, 2708, 2557 and 2437 mF cm−2 at current density of 3, 5, 11, 22 and 30 mA cm−2, respectively. In addition, the mass capacitance of 462 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1 is achieved for MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC. The specific capacitances of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC, MoS2/HCNT@CC and MoS2/CC are provided in Fig. 7d for comparison. The capacitance values from high to low are the MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC, MoS2/HCNT@CC and MoS2/CC. The excellent energy storage performance of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC is attributed to the synergy provided by the N-GQDs and HCNT. N-GQDs inserted into the layers of MoS2 sheets have induced and exposed more active sites for pseudocapacitance contributions of MoS2 materials and HCNT provides effective conductive channel, resulting in higher specific capacitance of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC. The effect of different reaction time (16, 20 and 24 h) on the capacitance properties of the MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC is shown in Fig. 7e. Clearly, MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC prepared at reaction time of 24 h has a higher specific capacitance. Thus, the optimal reaction time was 24 h. Furthermore, the capacitance properties of the MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC with different mass ratio of N-GQDs and HCNT are provided in Fig. 7f. The higher the mass ratio of N-GQDs, the lower the specific capacitance of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC. It may be due to the agglomeration of N-GQDs, which cannot be evenly dispersed between the MoS2 nanosheet layers, which hinders the ion insert and transport.

The specific capacitance of the MoS2-based electrodes is summarized in Table 2 . The MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC has higher specific capacitance and capacitance retention rate than the previously reported electrode materials, which confirms the synergistic effect of carbon materials and MoS2 effectively improves the electrochemical performance of the materials.

The capacitance contribution in MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC is further quantitatively analyzed by CV data. The diffusion and capacitance contributions can be separated using the following formula, where a, b are adjustable parameters and ν is the scan rate.
(4)
i = a
ν b
(5)
i
( V )
/
ν
1 / 2
=
a 1
ν
1 / 2
+
a 2
(6)
l o g i = b l o g ν + l o g a

The current response originated from diffusion and capacitive processes can be described at the specific potentials. Therefore, Fig. 8 a shows that when the scan rate is 0.1 mV s−1, the diffusion contribution of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC is as high as 26%, and the diffusion contribution is still 12% at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. (Fig. 8b). This result indicates that N-GQDs effectively enhance pseudocapacitance of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC by the rapid insertion/extraction of ions [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance in a two-electrode system
ce:section.ce:para: All-solid-state symmetric supercapacitors (ASSC) device was assembled by employing MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC as electrode active material and PVA-Na2SO4 was used as the gel electrolyte. The electrochemical performance of ASSC device is shown in Fig. 9 . Similar to the study in a three-electrode system, the CV curves of the ASSC devices are obtained within different scan voltage windows, as shown in Fig. 9a. When the voltage window is above 1.4 V, the cyclic voltammetry pattern shows obvious polarization phenomenon. Therefore, the potential for optimization can be determined as 1.2 V. In Fig. 9b, the shape of CV curve has no obvious change at different rates from 10 to 50 mV s−1, which confirms excellent rate stability of ASSC. Further, all GCD curves were approximately symmetrical triangle shapes (Fig. 9c), suggesting a highly reversible charge/discharge behavior in ASSC device [50]. Meanwhile, the specific capacitances of the MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC ASSC are 2097, 1627 and 1052 mF cm−2 at current densities of 7, 9 and 12 mA cm−2 based on the GCD curves, respectively. The comparison in Fig. 9d shows that the specific capacitance values of the MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC ASSC devices were much higher than those of MoS2@CC ASSC devices. As shown in Fig. 9e, the ASSC device displays great stability at 25 mA cm−2, the specific capacitance did not decrease obviously in the first 300 cycles, and remained about 86% of the original capacitor after 2500 cycles. Fig. 9f displays the Nyquist plots of the ASSC devices before and after 2500 cycles. The values of Rs increase from initial 3.4 Ω to 3.9 Ω. Compared with other symmetric devices reported previously, MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC ASSC devices has advantage in the specific capacitance and cyclic stability.

The flexibility and mechanical stability of ASSC device are shown in Fig. 10 . Fig. The fabrication of MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC ASSC device is illustrated in Fig. 10a. Clearly, the ASSC can be bended at different way, as shown in Fig. 10b. Fig. 10c-d show the CV curves of ASSC device at different bending degrees at a scanning rate of 10 mV s−1. It can be clearly seen that the shape of CV curves has no change for ASSC device in twisted or bent state, even at a bending angle of 180°, illustrating a good mechanical stability and great flexibility of the ASSC device. Fig. 10e shows the Ragone plot of the ASSC. When the power density is 5687 μW cm−2, the energy density is 673 μW h cm−2. And then the power density increased to 36,546 μW cm−2, the energy density remained at a high level of 294 μW h cm−2. Fig. 10f shows that an LED was lighted successfully by this ASSC device. the working voltage of the LED is 1.6–2.6 V, and the working current is 20 mA, suggesting that MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC as electrode material have good practical application in supercapacitors.

In summary, a binder-free three-dimensional layered MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC structure was prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. The MoS2 nanosheets grown on the surface of carbon cloth fibers and HCNT greatly increases the reaction surface area of the electrode material. Meanwhile, the HCNT acts as a bridge between the carbon cloth fibers; the tubular structure shortens the ion diffusion path. N-GQDs have abundant edge active sites and a large number of functional groups, which play an important role in improving pseudo-capacitance performance. The effective combination of carbon materials also significantly increases the conductivity of hybrid nanomaterials. As a result, the obtained MoS2/HCNT/N-GQDs@CC electrode has high specific capacitance of 3360 mF cm−2 at 3 mA cm−2. Furthermore, high capacitance retention of 89% is maintained after 2500 charging/discharging cycles. On the other hand, the symmetrical device shows a specific energy density of 673 μW h cm−2 and a power density of 5687μW cm−2. The demonstration of lighting up LED bulb shows great potential for application in small wearable electronic products. In addition, this unique vertical layered heterogeneity is a promising support material that can be used to support metal nanoparticles to improve the storage performance of pseudo capacitors. It provides new ideas for the design and fabrication of composite materials with high charge storage performance.

Hao Wu: Investigation, Writing- Original draft preparation. Zengsheng Guo: Investigation. Ming Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Guanghui Hu: Formal analysis, Visualization. Tao Tang: Resources, Visualization. Jianfeng Wen: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Xinyu Li: Project administration. Haifu Huang: Visualization, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11764011, 11604061, 11664007 and 51702059), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2018GXNSFAA281191, 2018GXNSFAA050014 and 2017GXNSFBA198131).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2021.137758.
